review_text: A 'must-see', aye, for those who like real, spectacular epics, no doubt about that. Photography and musical score are impeccable. Acting by Peter O'Toole and Anthony Quinn a bit overdone at times, but Omar Sharif, Alec Guinness, Jack Hawkins are remarkable, as Claude Rains, Arthur Kennedy and Anthony Quayle in secondary roles. David Lean knew how to make these films.

The location filming and staging by the crew really gave us some of the most incredible wide angle and landscape shots in cinema history. Was not aware at all that this was a biopic based on a real soldier. It maybe help explains what I thought was a diminished 3rd act comparatively. It was not shocking to read that Frank Herbert took inspiration from this movie for his Dune novels, it became pretty evident about an hour in that the lead characters shared similar qualities.

This film is a masterpiece of cinema. directed by David Lean, tells the epic tale of T.E. Lawrence's trip through the Arabian desert during World War I and has breathtaking cinematography and a standout performance by Peter O'Toole. The film is a must-see classic because of its expansive settings and engrossing narrative.

Brilliant film Great acting brilliant photography and film score. David lean deserves his inspirational impact on the likes of steven spielberg and other film directors. Anyone who gives it a lower rating obviously does not have any understanding of what David Lean has achieved in directing this film. Peter o'toole performance is outstanding and his not winning the oscar for best actor is to my mind one of the greatest traversities in Oscar History. Fully deserving of 7 oscar wins but should have won more. I also feel that a lot of films today are just aimed at much younger people who have have no understanding of History which is an incredibly important subject that you learn in school. It seems to me that anyone giving it a low score does not understand the films cultural impact or the importance of the quality and content of the film.

An impressive and highly influential historical epic. The film has such a grand scale with its vast landscape cinematography, large number of extras crowding the screen, and a sweeping heavily inspirational orchestral soundtrack. The titular character Lawrence is very quirky, clever, humble, and charming. His introduction is subversive with the opening scene reveals an older Lawrence dies a rather mundane death from a motorcycle crash after a lifetime of exotic adventures in WWI. During his wartime escapades in Arabia, Lawrence is a passionate leader and uses his emotional intelligence to help create a unified resistance with genuine collaborations between the different Arabic tribes to help defeat the ottoman empire with the promise of freedom from European control. However, the film is self-aware of the implications and pokes fun at the white savior trope during a few key debate scenes. From the get-go, the tribes have legitimate concerns that England will colonize Arabia after "helping" them defeat the Turks in WWI. There's also solid discourse about technological and geographic luck influencing European privilege and colonialism with banter that "The British are great because of our discipline" refuted with "Because they have guns and a navy". The movie is incredibly long, but the pacing, direction, and character work is so damn good that it just breezes by so quickly. I could watch them just banter and ride camels across the desert all day. And this is all given a great payoff with the epic train heist scenes and some funny commentary with the Chicago journalist. Lawrence and Arbia succeed in defeating the Turks, but questions arise, with different tribes now having to govern together and Britain and France breaking their agreement with looming threats of taking over Arbia as the new colonial successors to the ottoman empire in the aftermath of WWI. The film ends on a bittersweet note, but not without applaud for everyone's heroism. It's an epic war film that influenced an entire generation of filmmakers and definitely lives up to the hype.

Beautiful looking, depending on your tolerance of desert landscapes. Remarkable performance by Peter O’Toole of a shameless self promoter who believes his own mythology. Frames war as a story that needs to be packaged as an adventure of honorable heroes, while clearly showing it is anything but. The depicted meddling of Western powers into Arab affairs continue to ripple through history.

This film is beautifully shot, powerfully scored, and the first half is fantastic. But in the second half of the film, a little after they take Aqaba, the story itself seems to lose direction and purpose. The pace of the story really picks up; the motivation for the character's actions are not always clear--we never really get a clear understanding of who Lawrence is, why he wants what he wants, and why we, as an audience, should care. There is not enough character development for us to really care about any of the secondary characters either. The first half of the film is excellent and there are some compelling aspects of the second half, but the story itself is just not that good. The ending is interesting, but anticlimactic and unsatisfying. Being held in such high regard, ultimately, it was disappointing. In the scheme of things, the film left me wondering if TE Lawrence's existence mattered at all. What did he accomplish, really, and what difference did it make? This might sound cold, but it is not clear to me if this is a figure worth remembering--not to mention, immortalizing in a four-hour epic.

227 minutes of greatness! I've always wanted to watch 'Lawrence of Arabia' given its standing as being one of the greats, though that rather long run time always put it on my subconscious back-burner. At last, I rooted out some time for it and man it was a well spent near four hours! I'm not going to say its length is ignorable, though it truly doesn't feel like a 3hr 47min movie - so every credit to all involved. Peter O'Toole's performance is undoubtedly outstanding, I can't really add anything more to what I'm sure many, many others have noted down the decades - he is, put simply, brilliant. Other strong performers are Alec Guinness, Anthony Quinn, Omar Sharif and, albeit more minorly, Claude Rains. No-one on the cast puts a foot wrong, as expected. The cinematography throughout is also absolutely exquisite, the entire look and feel of the film is just stunning. There is much more to say about this but I'll leave that to those that have already said such things. I'm just here to watch great movies and this 1962 picture is unequivocally one of those. Quite evidently a classic, no doubt about it.

Wonderful cinematography, great sets and impressive action scenes. Worth watching for that alone. But the movie drags a little over it's huge runtime and seems to lack focus. The script was a bit on the theatrical side, especially later in the movie... the characters more focused on saying something profound than communicating. Also a pity they couldn't find Arabs to play all the Arab roles. Alec Guinness in particular was rather conspicuous. And the titular character was quite odd. Not sure what they were trying to say about Lawrence with Peter O'Tools muddled, half-bewildered interpretation. Probably no reflection of the historical person. Overall I loved the eye candy but not a huge fan of the character arc or story line.

You don't just feel the weight of the movie, but that of the entire production behind it. It's like you can almost hear the sand grinding up the equipment, smell the sweat of everyone involved and feel the long hours under the scorching sun. This is one for the ages.

I recently found out about the good people at AMC Theaters have been releasing classics on the big screen. All my life i heard about peoples list of the best movies ever made. This movie was always on everyone's list. I finally saw it on the big screen at my local AMC Theater. AND OH MY GOD. i was not ready for how great this movie is. The score was awesome, so loud in AMC's awesome sound system. i could hear all the instruments playing. This is movie is old school, no CGI, no effects...the story was well told and i learned a lot about society and people from this movie. It really is one of the best movies ever made. the 4hrs went by so fast, i was so intrigued by it. Deff need to see it again to understand it more but highly recommend this movie to anyone who is curious about this era in cinema. I also recommend it to anyone who just wants to see a damn good movie.
